question:
5. \( a_{1}, b_{1}, c_{1} \) are positive numbers whose sum is 1 , and for \( n=1,2, \ldots \) we define
\[
a_{n+1}=a_{n}^{2}+2 b_{n} c_{n}, b_{n+1}=b_{n}^{2}+2 c_{n} a_{n}, c_{n+1}=c_{n}^{2}+2 a_{n} b_{n} .
\]

Show that \( a_{n}, b_{n}, \boldsymbol{c}_{n} \) approach limits as \( n \rightarrow \infty \) and find these limits.
solution:
First Solution. First note that
\[
a_{n+1}+b_{n+1}+c_{n+1}=\left(a_{n}+b_{n}+c_{n}\right)^{2}
\]
so \( a_{k}+b_{k}+c_{k}=1 \) for all \( k \) by induction. Also it is clear that the \( a_{n} \) 's, \( b_{n} \) 's, and \( c_{n} \) 's are all positive.
Define \( E_{n}=\max \left(a_{n}, b_{n}, c_{n}\right) \) and \( F_{n}=\min \left(a_{n}, b_{n}, c_{n}\right) \). We will show
(1)
\[
\begin{array}{c}
F_{1} \leq F_{2} \leq F_{3} \leq \cdots \leq F_{n} \leq F_{n+1} \leq \cdots \\
\leq E_{n+1} \leq E_{n} \leq \cdots \leq E_{3} \leq E_{2} \leq E_{1}
\end{array}
\]
and also that
(2)
\[
\lim _{n-\infty}\left(E_{n}-F_{n}\right)=0 .
\]

It follows from (1) and (2) that \( E_{n} \) decreases weakly to some limit \( L \) and that \( F_{n} \) increases weakly to the same limit \( L \). Since \( F_{n} \leq a_{n} \leq E_{n} \), this implies \( a_{n} \rightarrow L \). Similarly \( b_{n} \rightarrow L \) and \( c_{n} \rightarrow L \). Then since \( a_{n}+b_{n}+c_{n} \) \( =1 \), we have \( L=\frac{1}{3} \).

To prove (1), assume \( a_{n} \geq b_{n} \geq c_{n} \) for some value of \( n \), then
(3)
\[
\begin{array}{l}
a_{n+1}=a_{n}{ }^{2}+b_{n} c_{n}+b_{n} c_{n} \\
b_{n+1}=a_{n} c_{n}+b_{n}^{2}+a_{n} c_{n} \\
c_{n+1}=a_{n} b_{n}+a_{n} b_{n}+c_{n}^{2} .
\end{array}
\]

In all equations in (3), the right member is less than or equal to \( a_{n}{ }^{2}+ \) \( a_{n} b_{n}+a_{n} c_{n}=a_{n} \), and greater than or equal to \( a_{n} c_{n}+b_{n} c_{n}+c_{n}^{2}=c_{n} \). Hence \( E_{n+1} \leq E_{n} \) and \( F_{n+1} \geq F_{n} \), which proves (1).
To prove (2), we again assume \( a_{n} \geq b_{n} \geq c_{n} \) for some \( n \). Set
\[
\begin{array}{l}
a_{n}-b_{n}=\alpha \geq 0 \\
b_{n}-c_{n}=\beta \geq 0 \\
a_{n}-c_{n}=\delta=\alpha+\beta \geq 0 .
\end{array}
\]

Then
\( \left|a_{n+1}-b_{n+1}\right|=\left|a_{n}-b_{n}\right|\left|a_{n}+b_{n}-2 c_{n}\right|=\alpha(\delta+\beta)=(\delta-\beta)(\delta+\beta) \leq \delta^{2} \) \( \left|a_{n+1}-c_{n+1}\right|=\left|a_{n}-c_{n}\right|\left|a_{n}+c_{n}-2 b_{n}\right|=\delta|\alpha-\beta| \leq \delta(\alpha+\beta)=\delta^{2} \) \( \left|c_{n+1}-b_{n+1}\right|=\left|b_{n}-c_{n}\right|\left|2 a_{n}-b_{n}-c_{n}\right|=\beta(\alpha+\delta) \leq(\delta-\alpha)(\delta+\alpha) \leq \delta^{2} \).

This set of inequalities shows that
\[
E_{n+1}-F_{n+1} \leq\left(E_{n}-F_{n}\right)^{2}
\]
for all \( n \). Therefore
\[
E_{n+1}-F_{n+1} \leq\left(E_{1}-F_{1}\right)^{2^{n}} \quad \text { for all } n .
\]

Since we are given that \( E_{1}<1 \) and \( F_{1}>0 \), we have \( E_{1}-F_{1}<1 \), and (2) follows. This concludes the proof.

Remark. The proof shows that the result still holds if one of the numbers \( a_{1}, b_{1}, c_{1} \) is zero and the other two are positive, since then \( E_{1}-F_{1} \) \( <1 \).

Second Solution. Put
\[
\begin{aligned}
u_{n} & =a_{n}+b_{n}+c_{n} \\
v_{n} & =a_{n}+\omega b_{n}+\omega^{2} c_{n} \\
w_{n} & =a_{n}+\omega^{2} b_{n}+\omega c_{n}
\end{aligned}
\]
where \( \omega \) is a complex cube root of unity. The recursion becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
u_{n+1} & =u_{n}^{2} \\
v_{n+1} & =w_{n}^{2} \\
w_{n+1} & =v_{n}^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( u_{1}=1 \), it follows that \( u_{n}=1 \) for all \( n \). Moreover we see by induction that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{v}_{n+1} & =\boldsymbol{v}_{1} 2^{2^{n}} \text { or } w_{1}^{2^{n}} \\
\boldsymbol{w}_{n+1} & =w_{1}^{2^{2}} \text { or } \boldsymbol{v}_{1^{2^{n}}}
\end{aligned}
\]
depending on whether \( n \) is even or odd. Since \( \nu_{1} \) and \( w_{1} \) are convex combinations with positive coefficients of the three numbers \( 1, \omega, \omega^{2} \), it follows that \( \left|v_{1}\right|<1 \) and \( \left|w_{1}\right|<1 \). (Note. For this conclusion it is sufficient that at least two of the numbers \( a_{1}, b_{1}, c_{1} \) be positive.) Hence \( v_{n} \rightarrow 0 \) and \( w_{n} \rightarrow 0 \). Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
a_{n}=\frac{1}{3}\left(u_{n}+v_{n}+w_{n}\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
b_{n}=\frac{1}{3}\left(u_{n}+\omega^{2} v_{n}+\omega w_{n}\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
c_{n}=\frac{1}{3}\left(u_{n}+\omega v_{n}+\omega^{2} w_{n}\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}
\end{array}
\]

Remark. The general properties of the transformation
\[
S:(a, b, c) \rightarrow\left(a^{2}+2 b c, b^{2}+2 c a, c^{2}+2 a b\right)
\]
are worth some attention. The points \( (a, b, c) \) for which \( a+b+c=1 \) form a plane \( P \), and those having also non-negative coordinates fill an equilateral triangular region \( T \) in \( P \). \( S \) maps \( P \) into itself and \( T \) into itself, leaving the vertices of \( T \) fixed and carrying the rest of \( T \) into its interior. The function
\[
v=a+b \omega+c \omega^{2}
\]
maps \( P \) bijectively to the complex plane, and sends the circumcircle of \( T \) onto the unit circle. In terms of \( v, S \) takes the simple form \( v \rightarrow \bar{v}^{2} \). From this form it is obvious that \( S \) is exactly two-to-one on all of \( P \) except at the origin (of \( v \) ). Furthermore, when \( S \) is iterated, points interior to the unit circle move very rapidly toward the origin, which corresponds to the point \( \left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right) \) in \( P \).

Third Solution. Define \( 3 \times 3 \) matrices \( A_{n} \) by
\[
A_{n}=\left(\begin{array}{lll}
a_{n} & b_{n} & c_{n} \\
c_{n} & a_{n} & b_{n} \\
b_{n} & c_{n} & a_{n}
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The recursion now becomes
\[
A_{n+1}=\left(A_{n}^{T}\right)^{2}
\]
where the \( \boldsymbol{T} \) stands for transpose. Hence
\[
A_{n+1}=A_{1}^{2^{n}} \text { or }\left(A_{1}^{T}\right)^{2^{n}}
\]
according as \( \boldsymbol{n} \) is even or odd.
A row-stochastic matrix is a square matrix in which all entries are nonnegative and every row sums to one. It is proved in the theory of Markov processes that if \( M \) is a row-stochastic matrix with all entries positive, then \( \lim _{k-\infty} M^{k} \) exists and is a row-stochastic matrix with all rows identical. There is a similar result, of course, for column-stochastic matrices.

Now \( A_{1} \) is doubly stochastic (i.e., both row- and column-stochastic) hence \( \lim _{k} A_{1}{ }^{k} \) exists and is a doubly stochastic matrix with all rows identical and all columns identical. Hence \( \lim _{k-\infty} A_{1}{ }^{k} \) is the matrix \( B \) with all entries \( \frac{1}{3} \). Of course,
\[
\left(A_{1}{ }^{T}\right)^{k}=\left(A_{1}{ }^{k}\right)^{T}
\]
has the same limit. Hence it follows that \( A_{n} \rightarrow B \) and
\[
a_{n} \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}, b_{n} \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}, c_{n} \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} .
\]
Remark. This solution is closely related to the second solution because
the numbers \( u_{n}, v_{n} \), and \( w_{n} \) are the eigenvalues of the matrix \( A_{n} \).

descriptive_long.question:
5. \( seqaone, seqbone, seqcone \) are positive numbers whose sum is 1, and for \( indexn=1,2, \ldots \) we define
\[
seqanplus=seqan^{2}+2 seqbn seqcn,\; seqbnplus=seqbn^{2}+2 seqcn seqan,\; seqcnplus=seqcn^{2}+2 seqan seqbn .
\]

Show that \( seqan, seqbn, \boldsymbol{seqcn} \) approach limits as \( indexn \rightarrow \infty \) and find these limits.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. First note that
\[
seqanplus+seqbnplus+seqcnplus=\left(seqan+seqbn+seqcn\right)^{2}
\]
so \( seriesa_{indexk}+seriesb_{indexk}+seriesc_{indexk}=1 \) for all \( indexk \) by induction. Also it is clear that the \( seqan \)'s, \( seqbn \)'s, and \( seqcn \)'s are all positive.
Define \( maxval_{indexn}=\max \left(seqan, seqbn, seqcn\right) \) and \( minval_{indexn}=\min \left(seqan, seqbn, seqcn\right) \). We will show
(1)
\[
\begin{array}{c}
minone \leq minval_{2} \leq minval_{3} \leq \cdots \leq minval_{indexn} \leq minnplus \leq \cdots \\
\leq maxnplus \leq maxval_{indexn} \leq \cdots \leq maxval_{3} \leq maxval_{2} \leq maxone
\end{array}
\]
and also that
(2)
\[
\lim _{indexn-\infty}\left(maxval_{indexn}-minval_{indexn}\right)=0 .
\]

It follows from (1) and (2) that \( maxval_{indexn} \) decreases weakly to some limit \( limitval \) and that \( minval_{indexn} \) increases weakly to the same limit \( limitval \). Since \( minval_{indexn} \leq seqan \leq maxval_{indexn} \), this implies \( seqan \rightarrow limitval \). Similarly \( seqbn \rightarrow limitval \) and \( seqcn \rightarrow limitval \). Then since \( seqan+seqbn+seqcn=1 \), we have \( limitval=\frac{1}{3} \).

To prove (1), assume \( seqan \geq seqbn \geq seqcn \) for some value of \( indexn \), then
(3)
\[
\begin{array}{l}
seqanplus=seqan^{2}+seqbn seqcn+seqbn seqcn \\
seqbnplus=seqan seqcn+seqbn^{2}+seqan seqcn \\
seqcnplus=seqan seqbn+seqan seqbn+seqcn^{2} .
\end{array}
\]

In all equations in (3), the right member is less than or equal to \( seqan^{2}+seqan seqbn+seqan seqcn=seqan \), and greater than or equal to \( seqan seqcn+seqbn seqcn+seqcn^{2}=seqcn \). Hence \( maxnplus \leq maxval_{indexn} \) and \( minnplus \geq minval_{indexn} \), which proves (1).

To prove (2), we again assume \( seqan \geq seqbn \geq seqcn \) for some \( indexn \). Set
\[
\begin{array}{l}
seqan-seqbn=gapalpha \geq 0 \\
seqbn-seqcn=gapbeta \geq 0 \\
seqan-seqcn=gapdelta=gapalpha+gapbeta \geq 0 .
\end{array}
\]

Then
\( \left|seqanplus-seqbnplus\right|=\left|seqan-seqbn\right|\left|seqan+seqbn-2 seqcn\right|=gapalpha(gapdelta+gapbeta)=(gapdelta-gapbeta)(gapdelta+gapbeta) \leq gapdelta^{2} \)

\( \left|seqanplus-seqcnplus\right|=\left|seqan-seqcn\right|\left|seqan+seqcn-2 seqbn\right|=gapdelta|gapalpha-gapbeta| \leq gapdelta(gapalpha+gapbeta)=gapdelta^{2} \)

\( \left|seqcnplus-seqbnplus\right|=\left|seqbn-seqcn\right|\left|2 seqan-seqbn-seqcn\right|=gapbeta(gapalpha+gapdelta) \leq(gapdelta-gapalpha)(gapdelta+gapalpha) \leq gapdelta^{2}. \)

This set of inequalities shows that
\[
maxnplus-minnplus \leq\left(maxval_{indexn}-minval_{indexn}\right)^{2}
\]
for all \( indexn \). Therefore
\[
maxnplus-minnplus \leq\left(maxone-minone\right)^{2^{indexn}} \quad \text { for all } indexn .
\]

Since we are given that \( maxone<1 \) and \( minone>0 \), we have \( maxone-minone<1 \), and (2) follows. This concludes the proof.

Remark. The proof shows that the result still holds if one of the numbers \( seqaone, seqbone, seqcone \) is zero and the other two are positive, since then \( maxone-minone<1 \).

Second Solution. Put
\[
\begin{aligned}
sumvaru_{indexn} & =seqan+seqbn+seqcn \\
sumvarv_{indexn} & =seqan+omegaunit seqbn+omegaunit^{2} seqcn \\
sumvarw_{indexn} & =seqan+omegaunit^{2} seqbn+omegaunit seqcn
\end{aligned}
\]
where \( omegaunit \) is a complex cube root of unity. The recursion becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
sumvaru_{indexn+1} & =sumvaru_{indexn}^{2} \\
sumvarv_{indexn+1} & =sumvarw_{indexn}^{2} \\
sumvarw_{indexn+1} & =sumvarv_{indexn}^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( sumvaru_{1}=1 \), it follows that \( sumvaru_{indexn}=1 \) for all \( indexn \). Moreover we see by induction that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{sumvarv}_{indexn+1} & =\boldsymbol{sumvarv}_{1}^{2^{indexn}} \text { or } sumvarw_{1}^{2^{indexn}} \\
\boldsymbol{sumvarw}_{indexn+1} & =sumvarw_{1}^{2^{2}} \text { or } \boldsymbol{sumvarv}_{1}^{2^{indexn}}
\end{aligned}
\]
depending on whether \( indexn \) is even or odd. Since \( vseqone \) and \( wseqone \) are convex combinations with positive coefficients of the three numbers \( 1, omegaunit, omegaunit^{2} \), it follows that \( \left|vseqone\right|<1 \) and \( \left|wseqone\right|<1 \). (Note. For this conclusion it is sufficient that at least two of the numbers \( seqaone, seqbone, seqcone \) be positive.) Hence \( sumvarv_{indexn} \rightarrow 0 \) and \( sumvarw_{indexn} \rightarrow 0 \). Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
seqan=\frac{1}{3}\left(sumvaru_{indexn}+sumvarv_{indexn}+sumvarw_{indexn}\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
seqbn=\frac{1}{3}\left(sumvaru_{indexn}+omegaunit^{2} sumvarv_{indexn}+omegaunit sumvarw_{indexn}\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
seqcn=\frac{1}{3}\left(sumvaru_{indexn}+omegaunit sumvarv_{indexn}+omegaunit^{2} sumvarw_{indexn}\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}
\end{array}
\]

Remark. The general properties of the transformation
\[
transform:(seriesa,seriesb,seriesc) \rightarrow\left(seriesa^{2}+2 seriesb seriesc, seriesb^{2}+2 seriesc seriesa, seriesc^{2}+2 seriesa seriesb\right)
\]
are worth some attention. The points \( (seriesa,seriesb,seriesc) \) for which \( seriesa+seriesb+seriesc=1 \) form a plane \( planevar \), and those having also non-negative coordinates fill an equilateral triangular region \( trianglereg \) in \( planevar \). \( transform \) maps \( planevar \) into itself and \( trianglereg \) into itself, leaving the vertices of \( trianglereg \) fixed and carrying the rest of \( trianglereg \) into its interior. The function
\[
sumvarv=seriesa+seriesb omegaunit+seriesc omegaunit^{2}
\]
maps \( planevar \) bijectively to the complex plane, and sends the circumcircle of \( trianglereg \) onto the unit circle. In terms of \( sumvarv, transform \) takes the simple form \( sumvarv \rightarrow \bar{sumvarv}^{2} \). From this form it is obvious that \( transform \) is exactly two-to-one on all of \( planevar \) except at the origin (of \( sumvarv \) ). Furthermore, when \( transform \) is iterated, points interior to the unit circle move very rapidly toward the origin, which corresponds to the point \( \left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right) \) in \( planevar \).

Third Solution. Define \( 3 \times 3 \) matrices \( matrixn \) by
\[
matrixn=\left(\begin{array}{lll}
seqan & seqbn & seqcn \\
seqcn & seqan & seqbn \\
seqbn & seqcn & seqan
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The recursion now becomes
\[
matrixnplus=\left(matrixn^{T}\right)^{2}
\]
where the \( \mathbf{T} \) stands for transpose. Hence
\[
matrixnplus=matrixaone^{2^{indexn}} \text { or }\left(matrixaone^{T}\right)^{2^{indexn}}
\]
according as \( indexn \) is even or odd.
A row-stochastic matrix is a square matrix in which all entries are nonnegative and every row sums to one. It is proved in the theory of Markov processes that if \( matrixcapm \) is a row-stochastic matrix with all entries positive, then \( \lim _{indexk-\infty} matrixcapm^{indexk} \) exists and is a row-stochastic matrix with all rows identical. There is a similar result, of course, for column-stochastic matrices.

Now \( matrixaone \) is doubly stochastic (i.e., both row- and column-stochastic) hence \( \lim _{indexk} matrixaone^{indexk} \) exists and is a doubly stochastic matrix with all rows identical and all columns identical. Hence \( \lim _{indexk-\infty} matrixaone^{indexk} \) is the matrix \( matrixcapb \) with all entries \( \frac{1}{3} \). Of course,
\[
\left(matrixaone^{T}\right)^{indexk}=\left(matrixaone^{indexk}\right)^{T}
\]
has the same limit. Hence it follows that \( matrixn \rightarrow matrixcapb \) and
\[
seqan \rightarrow \frac{1}{3},\; seqbn \rightarrow \frac{1}{3},\; seqcn \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} .
\]

Remark. This solution is closely related to the second solution because
the numbers \( sumvaru_{indexn}, sumvarv_{indexn}, \) and \( sumvarw_{indexn} \) are the eigenvalues of the matrix \( matrixn \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. \( sunflowerone, raincoatone, envelopeone \) are positive numbers whose sum is 1 , and for \( lighthouse=1,2, \ldots \) we define
\[
sunflowernext = sunflowerseq^{2} + 2 raincoatseq envelopeseq, \quad raincoatnext = raincoatseq^{2} + 2 envelopeseq sunflowerseq, \quad envelopenext = envelopeseq^{2} + 2 sunflowerseq raincoatseq .
\]

Show that \( sunflowerseq, raincoatseq, \boldsymbol{envelopeseq} \) approach limits as \( lighthouse \rightarrow \infty \) and find these limits.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. First note that
\[
sunflowernext + raincoatnext + envelopenext = \left(sunflowerseq + raincoatseq + envelopeseq\right)^{2}
\]
so \( sunflower_{crocodile}+raincoat_{crocodile}+envelope_{crocodile}=1 \) for all \( crocodile \) by induction. Also it is clear that the \( sunflowerseq \)'s, \( raincoatseq \)'s, and \( envelopeseq \)'s are all positive.
Define \( thunderboltseq=\max \left(sunflowerseq, raincoatseq, envelopeseq\right) \) and \( waterfallseq=\min \left(sunflowerseq, raincoatseq, envelopeseq\right) \). We will show
(1)
\[
\begin{array}{c}
waterfallone \leq waterfall_{2} \leq waterfall_{3} \leq \cdots \leq waterfallseq \leq waterfallnext \leq \cdots \\
\leq thunderboltnext \leq thunderboltseq \leq \cdots \leq thunderbolt_{3} \leq thunderbolt_{2} \leq thunderboltone
\end{array}
\]
and also that
(2)
\[
\lim _{lighthouse-\infty}\left(thunderboltseq-waterfallseq\right)=0 .
\]

It follows from (1) and (2) that \( thunderboltseq \) decreases weakly to some limit \( gemstone \) and that \( waterfallseq \) increases weakly to the same limit \( gemstone \). Since \( waterfallseq \leq sunflowerseq \leq thunderboltseq \), this implies \( sunflowerseq \rightarrow gemstone \). Similarly \( raincoatseq \rightarrow gemstone \) and \( envelopeseq \rightarrow gemstone \). Then since \( sunflowerseq+raincoatseq+envelopeseq =1 \), we have \( gemstone=\frac{1}{3} \).

To prove (1), assume \( sunflowerseq \geq raincoatseq \geq envelopeseq \) for some value of \( lighthouse \), then
(3)
\[
\begin{array}{l}
sunflowernext = sunflowerseq^{2}+raincoatseq\,envelopeseq+raincoatseq\,envelopeseq \\
raincoatnext = sunflowerseq\,envelopeseq+raincoatseq^{2}+sunflowerseq\,envelopeseq \\
envelopenext = sunflowerseq\,raincoatseq+sunflowerseq\,raincoatseq+envelopeseq^{2} .
\end{array}
\]

In all equations in (3), the right member is less than or equal to \( sunflowerseq^{2}+sunflowerseq\,raincoatseq+sunflowerseq\,envelopeseq=sunflowerseq \), and greater than or equal to \( sunflowerseq\,envelopeseq+raincoatseq\,envelopeseq+envelopeseq^{2}=envelopeseq \). Hence \( thunderboltnext \leq thunderboltseq \) and \( waterfallnext \geq waterfallseq \), which proves (1).

To prove (2), we again assume \( sunflowerseq \geq raincoatseq \geq envelopeseq \) for some \( lighthouse \). Set
\[
\begin{array}{l}
sunflowerseq-raincoatseq=\labyrinth \geq 0 \\
raincoatseq-envelopeseq=\whirlwind \geq 0 \\
sunflowerseq-envelopeseq=\sandstone=\labyrinth+\whirlwind \geq 0 .
\end{array}
\]

Then
\( \left|sunflowernext-raincoatnext\right|=\left|sunflowerseq-raincoatseq\right|\left|sunflowerseq+raincoatseq-2\,envelopeseq\right|=\labyrinth(\sandstone+\whirlwind)=(\sandstone-\whirlwind)(\sandstone+\whirlwind) \leq \sandstone^{2} \)

\( \left|sunflowernext-envelopenext\right|=\left|sunflowerseq-envelopeseq\right|\left|sunflowerseq+envelopeseq-2\,raincoatseq\right|=\sandstone|\labyrinth-\whirlwind| \leq \sandstone(\labyrinth+\whirlwind)=\sandstone^{2} \)

\( \left|envelopenext-raincoatnext\right|=\left|raincoatseq-envelopeseq\right|\left|2\,sunflowerseq-raincoatseq-envelopeseq\right|=\whirlwind(\labyrinth+\sandstone) \leq(\sandstone-\labyrinth)(\sandstone+\labyrinth) \leq \sandstone^{2} .\)

This set of inequalities shows that
\[
thunderboltnext-waterfallnext \leq\left(thunderboltseq-waterfallseq\right)^{2}
\]
for all \( lighthouse \). Therefore
\[
thunderboltnext-waterfallnext \leq\left(thunderboltone-waterfallone\right)^{2^{lighthouse}} \quad \text { for all } lighthouse .
\]

Since we are given that \( thunderboltone<1 \) and \( waterfallone>0 \), we have \( thunderboltone-waterfallone<1 \), and (2) follows. This concludes the proof.

Remark. The proof shows that the result still holds if one of the numbers \( sunflowerone, raincoatone, envelopeone \) is zero and the other two are positive, since then \( thunderboltone-waterfallone <1 \).

Second Solution. Put
\[
\begin{aligned}
telescopeseq & =sunflowerseq+raincoatseq+envelopeseq \\
bicyclistseq & =sunflowerseq+pendulum \, raincoatseq+pendulum^{2}\,envelopeseq \\
chocolateseq & =sunflowerseq+pendulum^{2}\,raincoatseq+pendulum\,envelopeseq
\end{aligned}
\]
where \( pendulum \) is a complex cube root of unity. The recursion becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
telescope_{lighthouse+1} & = telescopeseq^{2} \\
bicyclist_{lighthouse+1} & = chocolateseq^{2} \\
chocolate_{lighthouse+1} & = bicyclistseq^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( telescope_{1}=1 \), it follows that \( telescopeseq=1 \) for all \( lighthouse \). Moreover we see by induction that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{bicyclist}_{lighthouse+1} & =\boldsymbol{bicyclistone}^{2^{lighthouse}} \text { or } chocolateone^{2^{lighthouse}} \\
\boldsymbol{chocolate}_{lighthouse+1} & =chocolateone^{2^{lighthouse}} \text { or } \boldsymbol{bicyclistone}^{2^{lighthouse}}
\end{aligned}
\]
depending on whether \( lighthouse \) is even or odd. Since \( bicyclistone \) and \( chocolateone \) are convex combinations with positive coefficients of the three numbers \( 1, pendulum, pendulum^{2} \), it follows that \( \left|bicyclistone\right|<1 \) and \( \left|chocolateone\right|<1 \). (Note. For this conclusion it is sufficient that at least two of the numbers \( sunflowerone, raincoatone, envelopeone \) be positive.) Hence \( bicyclistseq \rightarrow 0 \) and \( chocolateseq \rightarrow 0 \). Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
sunflowerseq=\frac{1}{3}\left(telescopeseq+bicyclistseq+chocolateseq\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
raincoatseq=\frac{1}{3}\left(telescopeseq+pendulum^{2} \, bicyclistseq+pendulum \, chocolateseq\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
envelopeseq=\frac{1}{3}\left(telescopeseq+pendulum \, bicyclistseq+pendulum^{2} \, chocolateseq\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}
\end{array}
\]

Remark. The general properties of the transformation
\[
pinecone:(sunflower, raincoat, envelope) \rightarrow\left(sunflower^{2}+2\,raincoat\,envelope, \; raincoat^{2}+2\,envelope\,sunflower, \; envelope^{2}+2\,sunflower\,raincoat\right)
\]
are worth some attention. The points \( (sunflower, raincoat, envelope) \) for which \( sunflower+raincoat+envelope=1 \) form a plane \( hemisphere \), and those having also non-negative coordinates fill an equilateral triangular region \( paintbrush \) in \( hemisphere \). \( pinecone \) maps \( hemisphere \) into itself and \( paintbrush \) into itself, leaving the vertices of \( paintbrush \) fixed and carrying the rest of \( paintbrush \) into its interior. The function
\[
bicyclist=sunflower+raincoat\,pendulum+envelope\,pendulum^{2}
\]
maps \( hemisphere \) bijectively to the complex plane, and sends the circumcircle of \( paintbrush \) onto the unit circle. In terms of \( bicyclist, pinecone \) takes the simple form \( bicyclist \rightarrow \bar{bicyclist}^{2} \). From this form it is obvious that \( pinecone \) is exactly two-to-one on all of \( hemisphere \) except at the origin (of \( bicyclist \) ). Furthermore, when \( pinecone \) is iterated, points interior to the unit circle move very rapidly toward the origin, which corresponds to the point \( \left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right) \) in \( hemisphere \).

Third Solution. Define \( 3 \times 3 \) matrices \( sailboatseq \) by
\[
sailboatseq=\left(\begin{array}{lll}
sunflowerseq & raincoatseq & envelopeseq \\
envelopeseq & sunflowerseq & raincoatseq \\
raincoatseq & envelopeseq & sunflowerseq
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The recursion now becomes
\[
sailboatnext=\left(sailboatseq^{T}\right)^{2}
\]
where the \( T \) stands for transpose. Hence
\[
sailboatnext=sailboatinitial^{2^{lighthouse}} \text { or }\left(sailboatinitial^{T}\right)^{2^{lighthouse}}
\]
according as \( lighthouse \) is even or odd.

A row-stochastic matrix is a square matrix in which all entries are nonnegative and every row sums to one. It is proved in the theory of Markov processes that if \( nightingale \) is a row-stochastic matrix with all entries positive, then \( \lim _{crocodile-\infty} nightingale^{crocodile} \) exists and is a row-stochastic matrix with all rows identical. There is a similar result, of course, for column-stochastic matrices.

Now \( sailboatinitial \) is doubly stochastic (i.e., both row- and column-stochastic) hence \( \lim _{crocodile} sailboatinitial^{crocodile} \) exists and is a doubly stochastic matrix with all rows identical and all columns identical. Hence \( \lim _{crocodile-\infty} sailboatinitial^{crocodile} \) is the matrix \( snowflake \) with all entries \( \frac{1}{3} \). Of course,
\[
\left(sailboatinitial^{T}\right)^{crocodile}=\left(sailboatinitial^{crocodile}\right)^{T}
\]
has the same limit. Hence it follows that \( sailboatseq \rightarrow snowflake \) and
\[
sunflowerseq \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}, \quad raincoatseq \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}, \quad envelopeseq \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} .
\]
Remark. This solution is closely related to the second solution because
the numbers \( telescopeseq, bicyclistseq \), and \( chocolateseq \) are the eigenvalues of the matrix \( sailboatseq \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. \( negatermone, zerotermone, voidtermone \) are positive numbers whose sum is 1 , and for \( fixedcounter=1,2, \ldots \) we define
\[
negatermnext=negasequence^{2}+2 zerosequence voidsequence,\quad zerotermnext=zerosequence^{2}+2 voidsequence negasequence,\quad voidtermnext=voidsequence^{2}+2 negasequence zerosequence .
\]

Show that \( negasequence, zerosequence, \boldsymbol{voidsequence} \) approach limits as \( fixedcounter \rightarrow \infty \) and find these limits.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. First note that
\[
negatermnext+zerotermnext+voidtermnext=\left(negasequence+zerosequence+voidsequence\right)^{2}
\]
so \( negavalue_{staticindex}+zerovalue_{staticindex}+voidvalue_{staticindex}=1 \) for all \( staticindex \) by induction. Also it is clear that the negavalue's, zerovalue's, and voidvalue's are all positive.

Define \( minimalmaxseq=\max \left(negasequence, zerosequence, voidsequence\right) \) and \( maximminseq=\min \left(negasequence, zerosequence, voidsequence\right) \). We will show
(1)
\[
\begin{array}{c}
maximalone \leq maximminseq_{2} \leq maximminseq_{3} \leq \cdots \leq maximminseq \leq maximminnext \leq \cdots \\
\leq minimalmaxnext \leq minimalmaxseq \leq \cdots \leq minimalmaxseq_{3} \leq minimalmaxseq_{2} \leq minimalone
\end{array}
\]
and also that
(2)
\[
\lim _{fixedcounter-\infty}\left(minimalmaxseq-maximminseq\right)=0 .
\]

It follows from (1) and (2) that minimalmaxseq decreases weakly to some limit initialpoint and that maximminseq increases weakly to the same limit initialpoint. Since \( maximminseq \leq negasequence \leq minimalmaxseq \), this implies \( negasequence \rightarrow initialpoint \). Similarly \( zerosequence \rightarrow initialpoint \) and \( voidsequence \rightarrow initialpoint \). Then since \( negasequence+zerosequence+voidsequence =1 \), we have \( initialpoint=\frac{1}{3} \).

To prove (1), assume \( negasequence \geq zerosequence \geq voidsequence \) for some value of fixedcounter, then
(3)
\[
\begin{array}{l}
negatermnext=negasequence^{2}+zerosequence voidsequence+zerosequence voidsequence \\
zerotermnext=negasequence voidsequence+zerosequence^{2}+negasequence voidsequence \\
voidtermnext=negasequence zerosequence+negasequence zerosequence+voidsequence^{2} .
\end{array}
\]

In all equations in (3), the right member is less than or equal to \( negasequence^{2}+ negasequence zerosequence+negasequence voidsequence=negasequence \), and greater than or equal to \( negasequence voidsequence+zerosequence voidsequence+voidsequence^{2}=voidsequence \). Hence \( minimalmaxnext \leq minimalmaxseq \) and \( maximminnext \geq maximminseq \), which proves (1).

To prove (2), we again assume \( negasequence \geq zerosequence \geq voidsequence \) for some fixedcounter. Set
\[
\begin{array}{l}
negasequence-zerosequence=omegaparam \geq 0 \\
zerosequence-voidsequence=alphaparam \geq 0 \\
negasequence-voidsequence=identityval=omegaparam+alphaparam \geq 0 .
\end{array}
\]

Then
\( \left|negatermnext-zerotermnext\right|=\left|negasequence-zerosequence\right|\left|negasequence+zerosequence-2 voidsequence\right|=omegaparam(identityval+alphaparam)=(identityval-alphaparam)(identityval+alphaparam) \leq identityval^{2} \)
\( \left|negatermnext-voidtermnext\right|=\left|negasequence-voidsequence\right|\left|negasequence+voidsequence-2 zerosequence\right|=identityval|omegaparam-alphaparam| \leq identityval(omegaparam+alphaparam)=identityval^{2} \)
\( \left|voidtermnext-zerotermnext\right|=\left|zerosequence-voidsequence\right|\left|2 negasequence-zerosequence-voidsequence\right|=alphaparam(omegaparam+identityval) \leq(identityval-omegaparam)(identityval+omegaparam) \leq identityval^{2} \).

This set of inequalities shows that
\[
minimalmaxnext-maximminnext \leq\left(minimalmaxseq-maximminseq\right)^{2}
\]
for all fixedcounter. Therefore
\[
minimalmaxnext-maximminnext \leq\left(minimalone-maximalone\right)^{2^{fixedcounter}} \quad \text { for all fixedcounter } .
\]

Since we are given that \( minimalone<1 \) and \( maximalone>0 \), we have \( minimalone-maximalone<1 \), and (2) follows. This concludes the proof.

Remark. The proof shows that the result still holds if one of the numbers negatermone, zerotermone, voidtermone is zero and the other two are positive, since then \( minimalone-maximalone <1 \).

Second Solution. Put
\[
\begin{aligned}
separatevarseq & =negasequence+zerosequence+voidsequence \\
realnumberseq & =negasequence+alphasymbol zerosequence+alphasymbol^{2} voidsequence \\
integercountseq & =negasequence+alphasymbol^{2} zerosequence+alphasymbol voidsequence
\end{aligned}
\]
where alphasymbol is a complex cube root of unity. The recursion becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
separatevar_{fixedcounter+1} & =separatevarseq^{2} \\
realnumber_{fixedcounter+1} & =integercountseq^{2} \\
integercount_{fixedcounter+1} & =realnumberseq^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( separatevar_{1}=1 \), it follows that \( separatevarseq=1 \) for all fixedcounter. Moreover we see by induction that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{realnumber}_{fixedcounter+1} & =\boldsymbol{realnumberone}^{2^{fixedcounter}} \text { or } integercountone^{2^{fixedcounter}} \\
\boldsymbol{integercount}_{fixedcounter+1} & =integercountone^{2^{fixedcounter}} \text { or } \boldsymbol{realnumberone}^{2^{fixedcounter}}
\end{aligned}
\]
depending on whether fixedcounter is even or odd. Since realnumberone and integercountone are convex combinations with positive coefficients of the three numbers 1, alphasymbol, alphasymbol^{2}, it follows that \( \left|realnumberone\right|<1 \) and \( \left|integercountone\right|<1 \). (Note. For this conclusion it is sufficient that at least two of the numbers negatermone, zerotermone, voidtermone be positive.) Hence realnumberseq \( \rightarrow 0 \) and integercountseq \( \rightarrow 0 \). Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
negasequence=\frac{1}{3}\left(separatevarseq+realnumberseq+integercountseq\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
zerosequence=\frac{1}{3}\left(separatevarseq+alphasymbol^{2} realnumberseq+alphasymbol integercountseq\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
voidsequence=\frac{1}{3}\left(separatevarseq+alphasymbol realnumberseq+alphasymbol^{2} integercountseq\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}
\end{array}
\]

Remark. The general properties of the transformation
\[
stillmapping:(negavalue, zerovalue, voidvalue) \rightarrow\left(negavalue^{2}+2 zerovalue voidvalue, zerovalue^{2}+2 voidvalue negavalue, voidvalue^{2}+2 negavalue zerovalue\right)
\]
are worth some attention. The points \( (negavalue, zerovalue, voidvalue) \) for which negavalue+zerovalue+voidvalue=1 form a plane totalspace, and those having also non-negative coordinates fill an equilateral triangular region circledomain in totalspace. stillmapping maps totalspace into itself and circledomain into itself, leaving the vertices of circledomain fixed and carrying the rest of circledomain into its interior. The function
\[
variance=a+ b \alphasymbol+ c \alphasymbol^{2}
\]
maps totalspace bijectively to the complex plane, and sends the circumcircle of circledomain onto the unit circle. In terms of variance, stillmapping takes the simple form variance \( \rightarrow \bar{variance}^{2} \). From this form it is obvious that stillmapping is exactly two-to-one on all of totalspace except at the origin (of variance). Furthermore, when stillmapping is iterated, points interior to the unit circle move very rapidly toward the origin, which corresponds to the point \( \left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right) \) in totalspace.

Third Solution. Define \( 3 \times 3 \) matrices scalarfieldseq by
\[
scalarfieldseq=\left(\begin{array}{lll}
negasequence & zerosequence & voidsequence \\
voidsequence & negasequence & zerosequence \\
zerosequence & voidsequence & negasequence
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The recursion now becomes
\[
scalarfieldnext=\left(scalarfieldseq^{\circledomain}\right)^{2}
\]
where the \( \boldsymbol{circledomain} \) stands for transpose. Hence
\[
scalarfieldnext=scalarfieldone^{2^{fixedcounter}} \text { or }\left(scalarfieldone^{\circledomain}\right)^{2^{fixedcounter}}
\]
according as \( \boldsymbol{fixedcounter} \) is even or odd.

A row-stochastic matrix is a square matrix in which all entries are nonnegative and every row sums to one. It is proved in the theory of Markov processes that if randommatrix is a row-stochastic matrix with all entries positive, then \( \lim _{staticindex-\infty} randommatrix^{staticindex} \) exists and is a row-stochastic matrix with all rows identical. There is a similar result, of course, for column-stochastic matrices.

Now scalarfieldone is doubly stochastic (i.e., both row- and column-stochastic) hence \( \lim _{staticindex} scalarfieldone^{staticindex} \) exists and is a doubly stochastic matrix with all rows identical and all columns identical. Hence \( \lim _{staticindex-\infty} scalarfieldone^{staticindex} \) is the matrix variablematrix with all entries \( \frac{1}{3} \). Of course,
\[
\left(scalarfieldone^{\circledomain}\right)^{staticindex}=\left(scalarfieldone^{staticindex}\right)^{\circledomain}
\]
has the same limit. Hence it follows that scalarfieldseq \( \rightarrow variablematrix \) and
\[
negasequence \rightarrow \frac{1}{3},\quad zerosequence \rightarrow \frac{1}{3},\quad voidsequence \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} .
\]
Remark. This solution is closely related to the second solution because the numbers separatevarseq, realnumberseq, and integercountseq are the eigenvalues of the matrix scalarfieldseq.

garbled_string.question:
5. \( qzxwvtnpon, hjgrkslair, mnplcxqeem \) are positive numbers whose sum is 1 , and for \( rtdhfsjo=1,2, \ldots \) we define
\[
njxqsltaqq=njxqslta^{2}+2 hxtrdovb slfrtqmh,\; hxtrdovbqq=hxtrdovb^{2}+2 slfrtqmh njxqslta,\; slfrtqmhqq=slfrtqmh^{2}+2 njxqslta hxtrdovb .
\]

Show that \( njxqslta, hxtrdovb, \boldsymbol{slfrtqmh} \) approach limits as \( rtdhfsjo \rightarrow \infty \) and find these limits.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. First note that
\[
njxqsltaqq + hxtrdovbqq + slfrtqmhqq =\left(njxqslta + hxtrdovb + slfrtqmh\right)^{2}
\]
so \( qzxwvtnp_{zkylxmcv}+hjgrksla_{zkylxmcv}+mnplcxqe_{zkylxmcv}=1 \) for all \( zkylxmcv \) by induction. Also it is clear that the \( njxqslta \)'s, \( hxtrdovb \)'s, and \( slfrtqmh \)'s are all positive.

Define \( uvbnchmrgh =\max \left(njxqslta, hxtrdovb, slfrtqmh\right) \) and \( sopexkliqk =\min \left(njxqslta, hxtrdovb, slfrtqmh\right) \). We will show
(1)
\[
\begin{array}{c}
sopexklibt \leq sopexkliqk \leq \cdots \leq sopexkliqk \leq sopexkliqkqq \leq \cdots \\
\leq uvbnchmrghqq \leq uvbnchmrgh \leq \cdots \leq uvbnchmrgh \leq uvbnchmrxz
\end{array}
\]
and also that
(2)
\[
\lim _{rtdhfsjo-\infty}\left(uvbnchmrgh - sopexkliqk\right)=0 .
\]

It follows from (1) and (2) that \( uvbnchmrgh \) decreases weakly to some limit \( opqlasdg \) and that \( sopexkliqk \) increases weakly to the same limit \( opqlasdg \). Since \( sopexkliqk \leq njxqslta \leq uvbnchmrgh \), this implies \( njxqslta \rightarrow opqlasdg \). Similarly \( hxtrdovb \rightarrow opqlasdg \) and \( slfrtqmh \rightarrow opqlasdg \). Then since \( njxqslta+hxtrdovb+slfrtqmh =1 \), we have \( opqlasdg =\frac{1}{3} \).

To prove (1), assume \( njxqslta \geq hxtrdovb \geq slfrtqmh \) for some value of \( rtdhfsjo \), then
(3)
\[
\begin{array}{l}
njxqsltaqq = njxqslta^{2}+hxtrdovb slfrtqmh+hxtrdovb slfrtqmh \\
hxtrdovbqq = njxqslta slfrtqmh+hxtrdovb^{2}+njxqslta slfrtqmh \\
slfrtqmhqq = njxqslta hxtrdovb+njxqslta hxtrdovb+slfrtqmh^{2} .
\end{array}
\]

In all equations in (3), the right member is less than or equal to \( njxqslta^{2}+ njxqslta hxtrdovb+njxqslta slfrtqmh=njxqslta \), and greater than or equal to \( njxqslta slfrtqmh+hxtrdovb slfrtqmh+slfrtqmh^{2}=slfrtqmh \). Hence \( uvbnchmrghqq \leq uvbnchmrgh \) and \( sopexkliqkqq \geq sopexkliqk \), which proves (1).

To prove (2), we again assume \( njxqslta \geq hxtrdovb \geq slfrtqmh \) for some \( rtdhfsjo \). Set
\[
\begin{array}{l}
njxqslta-hxtrdovb = gjxmoytr \geq 0 \\
hxtrdovb-slfrtqmh = wfrdlcaz \geq 0 \\
njxqslta-slfrtqmh = xnvyqskb = gjxmoytr + wfrdlcaz \geq 0 .
\end{array}
\]

Then
\( \left|njxqsltaqq - hxtrdovbqq\right|=|njxqslta-hxtrdovb||njxqslta+hxtrdovb-2 slfrtqmh|=gjxmoytr(xnvyqskb+wfrdlcaz)=(xnvyqskb-wfrdlcaz)(xnvyqskb+wfrdlcaz) \leq xnvyqskb^{2} \)

\( \left|njxqsltaqq - slfrtqmhqq\right|=|njxqslta-slfrtqmh||njxqslta+slfrtqmh-2 hxtrdovb|=xnvyqskb|gjxmoytr-wfrdlcaz| \leq xnvyqskb(gjxmoytr+wfrdlcaz)=xnvyqskb^{2} \)

\( \left|slfrtqmhqq - hxtrdovbqq\right|=|hxtrdovb-slfrtqmh||2 njxqslta-hxtrdovb-slfrtqmh|=wfrdlcaz(gjxmoytr+xnvyqskb) \leq(xnvyqskb-gjxmoytr)(xnvyqskb+gjxmoytr) \leq xnvyqskb^{2}. \)

This set of inequalities shows that
\[
uvbnchmrghqq - sopexkliqkqq \leq\left(uvbnchmrgh - sopexkliqk\right)^{2}
\]
for all \( rtdhfsjo \). Therefore
\[
uvbnchmrghqq - sopexkliqkqq \leq\left(uvbnchmrxz - sopexklibt\right)^{2^{rtdhfsjo}} \quad \text { for all } rtdhfsjo .
\]

Since we are given that \( uvbnchmrxz <1 \) and \( sopexklibt >0 \), we have \( uvbnchmrxz - sopexklibt <1 \), and (2) follows. This concludes the proof.

Remark. The proof shows that the result still holds if one of the numbers \( qzxwvtnpon, hjgrkslair, mnplcxqeem \) is zero and the other two are positive, since then \( uvbnchmrxz - sopexklibt <1 \).

Second Solution. Put
\[
\begin{aligned}
vaymdtruzm & = njxqslta + hxtrdovb + slfrtqmh \\
twzmcekrrt & = njxqslta + rqwupnze\, hxtrdovb + rqwupnze^{2} slfrtqmh \\
yqldfapijl & = njxqslta + rqwupnze^{2} hxtrdovb + rqwupnze\, slfrtqmh
\end{aligned}
\]
where \( rqwupnze \) is a complex cube root of unity. The recursion becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
vaymdtruzm_{rtdhfsjo+1} & = vaymdtruzm_{rtdhfsjo}^{2} \\
twzmcekrrt_{rtdhfsjo+1} & = yqldfapijl_{rtdhfsjo}^{2} \\
yqldfapijl_{rtdhfsjo+1} & = twzmcekrrt_{rtdhfsjo}^{2}
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( vaymdtruzm_{1}=1 \), it follows that \( vaymdtruzm_{rtdhfsjo}=1 \) for all \( rtdhfsjo \). Moreover we see by induction that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{twzmcekrrt}_{rtdhfsjo+1} & = \boldsymbol{twzmcekron}^{2^{rtdhfsjo}} \text { or } yqldfapion^{2^{rtdhfsjo}} \\
\boldsymbol{yqldfapijl}_{rtdhfsjo+1} & = yqldfapion^{2^{rtdhfsjo}} \text { or } \boldsymbol{twzmcekron}^{2^{rtdhfsjo}}
\end{aligned}
\]
depending on whether \( rtdhfsjo \) is even or odd. Since \( twzmcekron \) and \( yqldfapion \) are convex combinations with positive coefficients of the three numbers \( 1, rqwupnze, rqwupnze^{2} \), it follows that \( \left|twzmcekron\right|<1 \) and \( \left|yqldfapion\right|<1 \). (Note. For this conclusion it is sufficient that at least two of the numbers \( qzxwvtnpon, hjgrkslair, mnplcxqeem \) be positive.) Hence \( twzmcekrrt \rightarrow 0 \) and \( yqldfapijl \rightarrow 0 \). Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
njxqslta =\frac{1}{3}\left(vaymdtruzm+twzmcekrrt+yqldfapijl\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
hxtrdovb =\frac{1}{3}\left(vaymdtruzm+ rqwupnze^{2} twzmcekrrt + rqwupnze\, yqldfapijl\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \\
slfrtqmh =\frac{1}{3}\left(vaymdtruzm+ rqwupnze\, twzmcekrrt + rqwupnze^{2} yqldfapijl\right) \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}
\end{array}
\]

Remark. The general properties of the transformation
\[
lswkgnbv:(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mnplcxqe) \rightarrow\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+2 hjgrksla\, mnplcxqe, hjgrksla^{2}+2 mnplcxqe\, qzxwvtnp, mnplcxqe^{2}+2 qzxwvtnp\, hjgrksla\right)
\]
are worth some attention. The points \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mnplcxqe) \) for which \( qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+mnplcxqe=1 \) form a plane \( rpcxjnda \), and those having also non-negative coordinates fill an equilateral triangular region \( hqtmzxke \) in \( rpcxjnda \). \( lswkgnbv \) maps \( rpcxjnda \) into itself and \( hqtmzxke \) into itself, leaving the vertices of \( hqtmzxke \) fixed and carrying the rest of \( hqtmzxke \) into its interior. The function
\[
twzmcekr = qzxwvtnp + hjgrksla\, rqwupnze + mnplcxqe\, rqwupnze^{2}
\]
maps \( rpcxjnda \) bijectively to the complex plane, and sends the circumcircle of \( hqtmzxke \) onto the unit circle. In terms of \( twzmcekr, lswkgnbv \) takes the simple form \( twzmcekr \rightarrow \bar{twzmcekr}^{2} \). From this form it is obvious that \( lswkgnbv \) is exactly two-to-one on all of \( rpcxjnda \) except at the origin (of \( twzmcekr \) ). Furthermore, when \( lswkgnbv \) is iterated, points interior to the unit circle move very rapidly toward the origin, which corresponds to the point \( \left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right) \) in \( rpcxjnda \).

Third Solution. Define \( 3 \times 3 \) matrices \( cebltowmgh \) by
\[
cebltowmgh=\left(\begin{array}{lll}
njxqslta & hxtrdovb & slfrtqmh \\
slfrtqmh & njxqslta & hxtrdovb \\
hxtrdovb & slfrtqmh & njxqslta
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The recursion now becomes
\[
cebltowmghqq =\left(cebltowmgh^{hqtmzxke}\right)^{2}
\]
where the \( \boldsymbol{hqtmzxke} \) stands for transpose. Hence
\[
cebltowmghqq = cebltowmon^{2^{rtdhfsjo}} \text { or }\left(cebltowmon^{hqtmzxke}\right)^{2^{rtdhfsjo}}
\]
according as \( \boldsymbol{rtdhfsjo} \) is even or odd.
A row-stochastic matrix is a square matrix in which all entries are nonnegative and every row sums to one. It is proved in the theory of Markov processes that if \( fevdizrj \) is a row-stochastic matrix with all entries positive, then \( \lim _{zkylxmcv-\infty} fevdizrj^{zkylxmcv} \) exists and is a row-stochastic matrix with all rows identical. There is a similar result, of course, for column-stochastic matrices.

Now \( cebltowmon \) is doubly stochastic (i.e., both row- and column-stochastic) hence \( \lim _{zkylxmcv} cebltowmon^{zkylxmcv} \) exists and is a doubly stochastic matrix with all rows identical and all columns identical. Hence \( \lim _{zkylxmcv-\infty} cebltowmon^{zkylxmcv} \) is the matrix \( kasmnhpu \) with all entries \( \frac{1}{3} \). Of course,
\[
\left(cebltowmon^{hqtmzxke}\right)^{zkylxmcv}=\left(cebltowmon^{zkylxmcv}\right)^{hqtmzxke}
\]
has the same limit. Hence it follows that \( cebltowmgh \rightarrow kasmnhpu \) and
\[
njxqslta \rightarrow \frac{1}{3},\; hxtrdovb \rightarrow \frac{1}{3},\; slfrtqmh \rightarrow \frac{1}{3}.
\]
Remark. This solution is closely related to the second solution because the numbers \( vaymdtruzm, twzmcekrrt, \) and \( yqldfapijl \) are the eigenvalues of the matrix \( cebltowmgh \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let non-negative real numbers x_0, y_0, z_0 satisfy
	x_0 + y_0 + z_0 = 1, and at least two of x_0 , y_0 , z_0 are positive.
For n = 0,1,2,\ldots  define recursively
	x_{n+1} = x_n^{2} + 2 y_n z_n ,
	y_{n+1} = y_n^{2} + 2 z_n x_n ,
	z_{n+1} = z_n^{2} + 2 x_n y_n .
(a) Prove that each of the three sequences (x_n), (y_n), (z_n) converges.
(b) Find their common limit.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use the shorthand
	S(a,b,c) := ( a^2+2bc , b^2+2ca , c^2+2ab ).
Put
	E_n := max{x_n , y_n , z_n}, F_n := min{x_n , y_n , z_n}, \Delta _n := E_n - F_n.

Step 1 - The sum is preserved
--------------------------------
A direct calculation shows
	x_{n+1}+y_{n+1}+z_{n+1}=x_n^2+y_n^2+z_n^2+2(y_nz_n+z_nx_n+x_ny_n)=(x_n+y_n+z_n)^2.
Because x_0+y_0+z_0=1, induction yields
	x_n + y_n + z_n = 1 for every n \geq  0.                                   (1)
In particular 0 \leq  x_n, y_n, z_n \leq  1.

Step 2 - Every coordinate remains between the current minimum and maximum
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Claim. For any non-negative a,b,c with a+b+c=1,
	min{a,b,c} \leq  S(a,b,c)_k \leq  max{a,b,c} (k=1,2,3).                      (2)

Upper bound in (2).
Let M = max{a,b,c}.  We show a^2+2bc \leq  M when M=a (the other two coordinates are analogous).
Because b+c = 1-a and bc \leq  (b+c)^2/4 = (1-a)^2/4,

a^2+2bc \leq  a^2 + (1-a)^2/2 = a^2 + (1-2a+a^2)/2 = (3a^2-2a+1)/2.
Hence it suffices to prove
	(3a^2-2a+1)/2 \leq  a  \Leftrightarrow  3a^2-4a+1 \leq  0.
The quadratic 3t^2-4t+1 has roots 1/3 and 1, and is non-positive exactly on the interval [1/3,1].
Since M=a \geq  1/3 (the largest of three numbers whose sum is 1 is at least 1/3), the desired inequality holds.

Lower bound in (2).
Let m = min{a,b,c} and assume m=a.  Write b=a+p, c=a+q with p,q\geq 0.  Using b+c=1-a we have a\leq 1/3.  Then

a^2+2bc-a = a(a-1)+2(a+p)(a+q) = a(1-3a)+2a(p+q)+2pq \geq  0,
because a\leq 1/3 and the last two terms are non-negative.  Thus a^2+2bc \geq  a = m.  The other coordinates are treated identically.

Therefore (2) is proved, and we deduce
	E_{n+1} \leq  E_n and F_{n+1} \geq  F_n for every n.                      (3)
Consequently (E_n) is decreasing and bounded below, while (F_n) is increasing and bounded above; both converge.  Write
	lim_{n\to \infty } E_n = E, lim_{n\to \infty } F_n = F with 0 \leq  F \leq  E \leq  1.        (4)

Step 3 - Quadratic contraction of the spread
-------------------------------------------
Without loss of generality suppose x_n \geq  y_n \geq  z_n.  Put
	\alpha  = x_n - y_n \geq  0, \beta  = y_n - z_n \geq  0, \delta  = \alpha  + \beta  = \Delta _n.
A routine algebra gives
	|x_{n+1} - y_{n+1}| \leq  \delta ^2,
	|y_{n+1} - z_{n+1}| \leq  \delta ^2,
	|x_{n+1} - z_{n+1}| \leq  \delta ^2.
Hence
	\Delta _{n+1} \leq  \Delta _n^2.                                                      (5)
Because at least two of the initial numbers are positive, E_0<1, so 0<\Delta _0<1.  Iterating (5) yields
	\Delta _n \leq  \Delta _0^{2^{n}} \to  0.                                                (6)

Step 4 - Identification of the common limit
------------------------------------------
Equation (6) implies E = F, so (4) gives
	lim_{n\to \infty } E_n = lim_{n\to \infty } F_n =: L.                                  (7)
Because every coordinate is squeezed between F_n and E_n, (7) entails
	x_n \to  L, y_n \to  L, z_n \to  L.                                           (8)
Finally, letting n\to \infty  in (1) we get 1 = x_n + y_n + z_n \to  3L, hence
	L = 1/3.

Conclusion.
Each of the sequences (x_n), (y_n), (z_n) converges, and
	lim_{n\to \infty } x_n = lim_{n\to \infty } y_n = lim_{n\to \infty } z_n = 1/3.

(The proof remains valid if exactly one of the initial numbers is zero, provided the other two are positive.)

core_steps: Sum invariance: prove a_n + b_n + c_n = 1 for all n
Positivity ⇒ define E_n = max, F_n = min; show F_n ↑, E_n ↓
Since 0 ≤ E_1 − F_1 < 1, deduce E_n − F_n → 0
Sandwich: F_n ≤ a_n,b_n,c_n ≤ E_n ⇒ each converges to common L, and by the sum L = 1/3

slot1.description: Allow the initial triple to be non-negative with at least two positive instead of all strictly positive; the proof still works because E_1 − F_1 < 1 remains true.

slot2.description: Begin indexing at n = 0 (or any fixed starting index) rather than n = 1; only notational, the recursion and argument are unchanged.

slot3.description: Rename or permute the variables (a, b, c) in the statement and proof; symmetry means the reasoning is unaffected.
slot3.original: Variables are labelled a, b, c in that order
